did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a circular 2D causal auto-regressive adaptive random (2DSCAR) field model, a statistical machine learning method, to extract multispectral rotationally invariant textural features for skin lesion classification (melanoma recognition). The authors explicitly compare this method's performance against deep neural networks (CNNs) on the ISIC database.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper introduces a novel circular 2D causal auto-regressive adaptive random (2DSCAR) field model for melanoma recognition, presents experimental results on the ISIC database (23,901 images), and compares its performance against deep neural networks. It is published in the proceedings of the 17th International Joint Conference on Computer Vision, Imaging and Computer Graphics Theory and Applications (VISIGRAPP 2022).

title: Melanoma Recognition
journal: Proceedings of the 17th International Joint Conference on Computer Vision, Imaging and Computer Graphics Theory and Applications (VISIGRAPP 2022)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of melanoma recognition by introducing a circular 2D causal auto-regressive adaptive random (2DSCAR) field model to extract multispectral rotationally invariant textural features for skin lesion classification. The authors evaluated their method on the open-source ISIC database, which comprises 23,901 dermoscopic images from five datasets (2018 JID Editorial, HAM10000, MSK, SONIC, and UDA). The classifier was tested using a leave-one-out approach on the original data without augmentation, achieving an overall accuracy of 86.3%. The results demonstrate that the proposed statistical machine learning method outperformed several deep neural network alternatives, such as AlexNet and ResNet50, while requiring significantly fewer parameters and less training data. The study concludes that the 2DSCAR model offers a computationally efficient and effective alternative to deep learning for skin cancer diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study classifies lesions into malignant, benign, and unknown categories. The malignant class includes melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and squamous cell carcinoma, particularly noted in the 2018 JID Editorial dataset which contains 37 melanomas, 40 basal cell carcinomas, and 23 squamous cell carcinomas. The overall dataset has 10% malignant images.

The study classifies lesions into malignant, benign, and unknown categories. The benign class constitutes 80% of the ISIC database used. Specific benign types are not enumerated in the text, though the HAM10000 dataset is described as containing 'common pigmented skin lesions'.
The study classifies lesions into malignant, benign, and unknown categories. The unknown class constitutes 10% of the ISIC database used. Figure 4 provides examples labeled 'u - unknown'.

all_task_types.details: The paper performs skin lesion categorization/classification into three discrete classes: benign, malignant, and unknown. The method uses a 1-nearest-neighbor classifier based on Jeffreys divergence of Gaussian distribution parameters extracted from 2DSCAR features. Accuracy is reported as 86.3% on the ISIC database.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Database (International Skin Imaging Collaboration Archive)
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dermoscopic skin image database composed of five constituent datasets: 2018 JID Editorial, HAM10000, MSK, SONIC, and UDA. The images were sourced from various clinical and research settings, including biopsied lesions from Caucasian patients in the southern US (2018 JID), digitized slides and digital dermatoscopy (HAM10000), SLR cameras and smartphones (MSK), Fujifilm and Nikon cameras (SONIC), and subsets with metadata (UDA).

use.training: Used for training the 2DSCAR model and 1-NN classifier via a leave-one-out approach on the original dataset without data augmentation.
use.validation: Used for validation via a leave-one-out approach on the original dataset without data augmentation.
use.testing: Used for testing via a leave-one-out approach on the original dataset without data augmentation.
use.other: Used for comparison with deep neural network results from Favole et al. (2020) which used an 80:20 hold-out split with data augmentation.

datasets_used.name: 2018 JID Editorial Dataset
datasets_used.description: A subset of the ISIC archive containing 100 sequentially biopsied cutaneous lesions (37 melanomas, 40 basal cell carcinomas, 23 squamous cell carcinomas) from Caucasian patients in the southern United States.

use.training: Included in the total ISIC dataset used for leave-one-out classification.
use.validation: Included in the total ISIC dataset used for leave-one-out classification.
use.testing: Included in the total ISIC dataset used for leave-one-out classification.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A subset of the ISIC archive containing 10,015 images of common pigmented skin lesions collected over 20 years, acquired via Nikon Coolscan 5000 ED scanner, MoleMax HD, or DermLite Foto camera.

use.training: Included in the total ISIC dataset used for leave-one-out classification.
use.validation: Included in the total ISIC dataset used for leave-one-out classification.
use.testing: Included in the total ISIC dataset used for leave-one-out classification.

datasets_used.name: MSK Dataset
datasets_used.description: A subset of the ISIC archive containing 3,918 images from five subsets, acquired using a dermoscopic attachment to a digital single-reflex lens (SLR) camera or a smartphone.

use.training: Included in the total ISIC dataset used for leave-one-out classification.
use.validation: Included in the total ISIC dataset used for leave-one-out classification.
use.testing: Included in the total ISIC dataset used for leave-one-out classification.

datasets_used.name: SONIC Dataset
datasets_used.description: A subset of the ISIC archive containing 9,251 images from SONIC Healthcare, Australia, acquired with Fujifilm FinePix S2 Pro and 176 Nikon D300 cameras.

use.training: Included in the total ISIC dataset used for leave-one-out classification.
use.validation: Included in the total ISIC dataset used for leave-one-out classification.
use.testing: Included in the total ISIC dataset used for leave-one-out classification.

datasets_used.name: UDA Dataset
datasets_used.description: A subset of the ISIC archive containing 617 images with two subsets, including melanoma and benign lesions with histopathological diagnosis or clinically benign history, containing metadata (patient age, diagnosis, gender, anatomic location).

use.training: Included in the total ISIC dataset used for leave-one-out classification.
use.validation: Included in the total ISIC dataset used for leave-one-out classification.
use.testing: Included in the total ISIC dataset used for leave-one-out classification.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To estimate the classification rate and evaluate the performance of the 2DSCAR classifier on the original dataset without data augmentation.
To serve as a symmetric similarity measure for comparing the multivariate Gaussian distribution parameters of the tested image against training samples in the 1-nearest-neighbor classifier.
To efficiently and analytically estimate the descriptive multispectral circular wide-sense Markov model statistics (parameters) from the image data.
Defined as the basis for the Jeffreys divergence to measure the non-symmetric similarity between probability distributions of compared images.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and precision via a leave-one-out cross-validation approach, and compared qualitatively with other studies.

ISIC Database (2018 JID Editorial, HAM10.notes: The 2DSCAR method achieved 86.3% accuracy on the original dataset without data augmentation using a leave-one-out approach. When augmented with flipped versions of malignant and unknown images (similar to Favole et al., 2020), accuracy improved to 95%. The 2DSCAR method used significantly fewer parameters (1.6 * 10^3) compared to CNNs (e.g., AlexNet: 6 * 10^7, ResNet50: 2.6 * 10^7). Hosny et al. reported the highest accuracy (98%) among compared methods but used pre-trained nets with transfer learning and unspecified training data.

architecture_used.main_model: Circular 2D causal auto-regressive adaptive random (2DSCAR) field model
architecture_used.pretraining: None; parameters are estimated analytically using Bayesian parameter estimation (conditional mean) from the training data itself.
architecture_used.input_size: Images subsampled to width of 512 pixels (aspect ratio preserved); stride of 2 for feature extraction.

architecture_details.model_type: Statistical machine learning / Markov Random Field
architecture_details.description: A multispectral rotationally invariant textural feature extraction model based on a circular wide-sense Markov model. It uses an octagonal traversing order (circular path) to estimate descriptive parameters. Features are derived from the estimated multivariate Gaussian distribution parameters (mean and covariance) of the image texture.
architecture_details.feature_extraction: Extracts approximately 40 features (depending on neighborhood size) representing the mean and covariance matrix of the Gaussian distribution governing the feature space.
architecture_details.neighborhood: Vertical and horizontal stride of 2; neighborhood support vector of multispectral pixels.

training_details.hyperparameters: Stride of 2; image width subsampling to 512 px; neighborhood size (determines number of features, approx 40).

classification_or_output_head.similarity_metric: Jeffreys divergence (symmetrized Kullback-Leibler divergence) between the Gaussian distribution parameters of the test image and training samples.
classification_or_output_head.output: Class label (Benign, Malignant, or Unknown) corresponding to the training sample with the lowest Jeffreys divergence.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Circular 2D causal auto-regressive adaptive random (2DSCAR) field model
preprocessing_techniques: Image subsampling to a width of 512 pixels while preserving aspect ratio
Vertical and horizontal stride of 2 for feature extraction
Data augmentation via flipping of malignant and unknown images (in comparative analysis with Favole et al., 2020)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Flipped versions of malignant and unknown images were added to the training set to improve accuracy, following the method used by Favole et al. (2020). This resulted in a test database containing 52% benign, 24% malignant, and 24% unknown images.

conclusions: The proposed circular 2D causal auto-regressive adaptive random (2DSCAR) field model achieves high accuracy for skin lesion categorization, outperforming several deep neural network alternatives while using significantly smaller training data.
The 2DSCAR method achieved an overall accuracy of 86.3% on the ISIC database using a leave-one-out approach on the original dataset without data augmentation.
When data augmentation (flipping of malignant and unknown images) was applied, the 2DSCAR method's accuracy improved to 95%, with sensitivity reaching 97-99.7% for malignant and unknown classes.

The 2DSCAR method requires significantly fewer parameters (1.6 × 10^3) compared to deep neural networks such as AlexNet (6 × 10^7), ResNet50 (2.6 × 10^7), and ARL-CNN50 (2.3 × 10^7), indicating it needs much less data for reliable model learning.

The method offers a computationally efficient alternative to deep learning, with training times orders of magnitude faster than CNNs and the ability to compare hundreds of thousands of image feature distributions per second.

The study demonstrates that statistical machine learning methods using multispectral rotationally invariant textural features can effectively compete with or exceed the performance of deep learning approaches in melanoma recognition tasks.
important_remarks: The dataset is highly imbalanced (80% benign, 10% malignant, 10% unknown), which may bias the classifier towards the majority class and lower diagnostic accuracy for malignant lesions.

The primary evaluation used a leave-one-out approach on the original dataset without data augmentation, whereas comparative deep learning studies (e.g., Favole et al., 2020) used an 80:20 hold-out split with data augmentation, potentially inflating the performance gap.

When data augmentation (flipping malignant and unknown images) was applied, accuracy improved to 95%, suggesting the method's performance is sensitive to training data balance and augmentation strategies.

Comparisons with some deep learning methods (ARL-CNN50, R-CNN, Hosny) are limited because those studies did not specify their training data or parameters precisely, making direct comparison difficult.

The study acknowledges that challenges such as viewing angles, body position, observation distance, resolution, and variability in acquisition technologies were not addressed and need future investigation.

The method relies on dermoscopic images from specific sources (e.g., Caucasian patients in the southern US for the 2018 JID Editorial subset), which may limit generalizability to other populations or non-dermoscopic clinical settings.

The 2DSCAR method uses significantly fewer parameters (1.6 × 10^3) compared to CNNs (e.g., AlexNet: 6 × 10^7), indicating lower data requirements but potentially less capacity to capture complex features compared to deep networks.
